Ronjan Basketball League

EST. 1998

A 100-season simulation recreating a program originally written in Visual Basic in 1998.

➜ Relocated to Monterrey Summit after Season 81
How It Works

A 100-season basketball universe — six Python files, ~900 lines, built with Claude Code

Competitive balance over time

No team stays dominant forever. Every win and loss shifts a team's quality rating up or down, creating in-season momentum. Each offseason, playoff teams reset to quality levels reflecting how far they went — a 7-game semifinal exit is worth more than a sweep. Non-playoff teams drift randomly, with big-market teams having a slight upward pull (talent magnet) and small-market teams a slight downward one. This produces realistic cycles of dynasty, decline, and rebuilding.

Offensive and defensive identities

Every team has an identity — a value from 0 (pure defense) to 1 (pure offense) — that affects how games are scored. Winning stabilizes that identity; losing makes a team more willing to change its approach. Champions entrench their style; perennial losers search for answers. A league-wide meta nudge means teams gradually absorb the style of whatever era they're playing in, making winning styles temporarily self-reinforcing.

League eras and rule changes

The league cycles through offensive and defensive eras organically — driven by what style of team is winning championships. Offensive eras play at a higher tempo (~110 ppg), defensive eras slow down (~90 ppg). Eras are self-reinforcing but naturally reverse via momentum decay and spring reversion. If an era runs too extreme for too long, a rule-change shock fires — yanking the meta back toward neutral, sometimes overcorrecting into the opposite extreme, just like the real game.

Market forces and relocation

Cities have market sizes that shape long-run team trajectories. Big markets attract talent and recover from down years more reliably. Small-market teams can compete and win, but sustained failure is harder to reverse. After 8+ consecutive losing seasons with repeated bottom-2 finishes, a team becomes eligible to relocate — blocked only by championship protection (20 seasons) or finals protection (10 seasons). Teams can't move to a city smaller than half their current market.

Fanbase and popularity

Every team has a popularity score anchored to its market baseline. Winning pushes it above that baseline — a small-market champion can build a national following far larger than their city would predict, like the real-world Green Bay effect. But sustained losing erodes it, progressively faster the longer the drought continues. Crucially, the miss-playoffs penalty only applies to teams already above their baseline: disappointment requires expectation. Teams that relocate carry 60% of their fanbase to the new city.

Playoff seeding and series dynamics

The top 8 teams enter a seeded bracket (1v8, 2v7, etc.) in best-of-7 series. Higher seeds earn a constant bonus in every game — not just home games — rewarding regular-season performance. Series length is tracked across all three rounds, producing realistic distributions of sweeps, 6-game series, and 7-game classics. The seed-vs-seed win grid reveals how much the bracket rewards or punishes regular-season position over the full 100-season run.

Built with Claude Code · Python · ~900 lines · seed 19

Recreating a Visual Basic program from 1998, one season at a time.
